For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

(*lingua Latina* or *Latinum*[[I]](#cite_note-IPA-1)) is a [classical language](/wiki/Classical_language "Classical language") belonging to the [Italic branch](/wiki/Italic_languages "Italic languages") of the [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). Latin was originally spoken by the [Latins](/wiki/Latins_(Italic_tribe) "Latins (Italic tribe)") in [Latium](/wiki/Latium "Latium") (now known as [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio")), the lower [Tiber](/wiki/Tiber "Tiber") area around [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), Italy.[[1]](#cite_note-2) Through the expansion of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), it became the dominant language in the [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula") and subsequently throughout the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). It has greatly influenced many languages, [including English](/wiki/Latin_influence_in_English "Latin influence in English"), having contributed [many words](/wiki/List_of_Latin_words_with_English_derivatives "List of Latin words with English derivatives") to the English [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon"), particularly after the [Christianisation of the Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Christianity_in_Anglo-Saxon_England "Christianity in Anglo-Saxon England") and the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"). Latin [roots](/wiki/Root_(linguistics) "Root (linguistics)") appear frequently in the technical vocabulary used by fields such as [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), [the sciences](/wiki/List_of_Latin_and_Greek_words_commonly_used_in_systematic_names "List of Latin and Greek words commonly used in systematic names"), [medicine](/wiki/List_of_medical_roots,_suffixes_and_prefixes "List of medical roots, suffixes and prefixes"), and [law](/wiki/List_of_Latin_legal_terms "List of Latin legal terms").

**Latin** (*lingua Latina* or *Latinum*[[I]](#cite_note-IPA-1)) is a [classical language](/wiki/Classical_language "Classical language") belonging to the [Italic branch](/wiki/Italic_languages "Italic languages") of the [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). Latin was originally spoken by the [Latins](/wiki/Latins_(Italic_tribe) "Latins (Italic tribe)") in [Latium](/wiki/Latium "Latium") (now known as [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio")), the lower [Tiber](/wiki/Tiber "Tiber") area around [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), Italy.[[1]](#cite_note-2) Through the expansion of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), it became the dominant language in the [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula") and subsequently throughout the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). It has greatly influenced many languages, [including English](/wiki/Latin_influence_in_English "Latin influence in English"), having contributed [many words](/wiki/List_of_Latin_words_with_English_derivatives "List of Latin words with English derivatives") to the English [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon"), particularly after the [Christianisation of the Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Christianity_in_Anglo-Saxon_England "Christianity in Anglo-Saxon England") and the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"). Latin [roots](/wiki/Root_(linguistics) "Root (linguistics)") appear frequently in the technical vocabulary used by fields such as [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), [the sciences](/wiki/List_of_Latin_and_Greek_words_commonly_used_in_systematic_names "List of Latin and Greek words commonly used in systematic names"), [medicine](/wiki/List_of_medical_roots,_suffixes_and_prefixes "List of medical roots, suffixes and prefixes"), and [law](/wiki/List_of_Latin_legal_terms "List of Latin legal terms").

By the late [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), [Old Latin](/wiki/Old_Latin "Old Latin") had evolved into standardised [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin"). [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") refers to the less prestigious [colloquial registers](/wiki/Colloquial_register "Colloquial register"), attested in inscriptions and some literary works such as those of the comic playwrights [Plautus](/wiki/Plautus "Plautus") and [Terence](/wiki/Terence "Terence")[[2]](#cite_note-3) and the author [Petronius](/wiki/Petronius "Petronius"). While often called a "dead language",[[3]](#cite_note-4) Latin did not undergo [language death](/wiki/Language_death "Language death"). Between the 6th and 9th centuries, natural [language change](/wiki/Language_change "Language change") in the vernacular Latin of different regions evolved into distinct [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"). After the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), Latin remained the [common language](/wiki/Common_language "Common language") of [international communication](/wiki/International_communication "International communication"), science, scholarship and academia in Europe into the early 19th century, by which time modern languages had supplanted it in common academic and political usage.

[Late Latin](/wiki/Late_Latin "Late Latin") is the literary form of the language from the 3rd century AD onward. No longer spoken as a native language, [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") was used across Western and [Catholic Europe](/wiki/Catholic_Europe "Catholic Europe") during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") as a working and literary language from the 9th century to the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), which then developed a classicising form, called [Renaissance Latin](/wiki/Renaissance_Latin "Renaissance Latin"). This was the basis for [Neo-Latin](/wiki/Neo-Latin "Neo-Latin"), which evolved during the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"). Latin was taught to be written and spoken at least until the late seventeenth century, when spoken skills began to erode; [Contemporary Latin](/wiki/Contemporary_Latin "Contemporary Latin") is generally studied to be read rather than spoken. [Ecclesiastical Latin](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Latin "Ecclesiastical Latin") remains the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") and the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").

[Latin grammar](/wiki/Latin_grammar "Latin grammar") is highly [fusional](/wiki/Fusional_language "Fusional language"), with classes of inflections for [case](/wiki/Noun_case "Noun case"), [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"), [person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person"), [gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), [tense](/wiki/Latin_tenses "Latin tenses"), [mood](/wiki/Grammatical_mood "Grammatical mood"), [voice](/wiki/Voice_(grammar) "Voice (grammar)"), and [aspect](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect"). The [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") is directly derived from the [Etruscan](/wiki/Etruscan_alphabet "Etruscan alphabet") and [Greek alphabets](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet").

## History

Main article: [History of Latin](/wiki/History_of_Latin "History of Latin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Linguistic_Landscape_of_Central_Italy.png/500px-Linguistic_Landscape_of_Central_Italy.png)](/wiki/File:Linguistic_Landscape_of_Central_Italy.png)

The linguistic landscape of central Italy at the beginning of Roman expansion

A number of phases of the language have been recognised, each distinguished by subtle differences in vocabulary, usage, spelling, and syntax. There are no hard and fast rules of classification; different scholars emphasise different features. As a result, the list has variants, as well as alternative names.

In addition to the historical phases, [Ecclesiastical Latin](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Latin "Ecclesiastical Latin") refers to the styles used by the writers of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") from [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") onward, as well as by [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") scholars.

### Old Latin

Main article: [Old Latin](/wiki/Old_Latin "Old Latin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Lapis-niger.jpg/250px-Lapis-niger.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lapis-niger.jpg)

The [Lapis Niger](/wiki/Lapis_Niger "Lapis Niger"), probably the oldest extant Latin inscription, from Rome, c. 600 BC during the semi-legendary [Roman Kingdom](/wiki/Roman_Kingdom "Roman Kingdom")

The earliest known form of Latin is Old Latin, also called Archaic or Early Latin, which was spoken from the [Roman Kingdom](/wiki/Roman_Kingdom "Roman Kingdom"), traditionally founded in 753 BC, through the later part of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), up to 75 BC, i.e. before the age of [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin").[[4]](#cite_note-5) It is attested both in inscriptions and in some of the earliest extant Latin literary works, such as the comedies of [Plautus](/wiki/Plautus "Plautus") and [Terence](/wiki/Terence "Terence"). The [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") was devised from the [Etruscan alphabet](/wiki/Etruscan_alphabet "Etruscan alphabet"). The writing later changed from what was initially either a [right-to-left](/wiki/Right-to-left_script "Right-to-left script") or a [boustrophedon](/wiki/Boustrophedon "Boustrophedon")[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) script to what ultimately became a strictly left-to-right script.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

### Classical Latin

Main article: [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin")

During the late republic and into the first years of the empire, from about 75 BC to AD 200, a new [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") arose, a conscious creation of the orators, poets, historians and other [literate](/wiki/Literate "Literate") men, who wrote the great works of [classical literature](/wiki/Classical_literature "Classical literature"), which were taught in [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") and [rhetoric](/wiki/Rhetoric "Rhetoric") schools. Today's instructional grammars trace their roots to such [schools](/wiki/Roman_school "Roman school"), which served as a sort of informal language academy dedicated to maintaining and perpetuating educated speech.[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)

### Vulgar Latin

Main article: [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin")

Philological analysis of Archaic Latin works, such as those of [Plautus](/wiki/Plautus "Plautus"), which contain fragments of everyday speech, gives evidence of an informal register of the language, Vulgar Latin (termed *sermo vulgi* 'the speech of the masses', by [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero")). Some linguists, particularly in the nineteenth century, believed this to be a separate language, existing more or less in parallel with the literary or educated Latin, but this is now widely dismissed.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

The term 'Vulgar Latin' remains difficult to define, referring both to informal speech at any time within the history of Latin, and the kind of informal Latin that had begun to move away from the written language significantly in the post-Imperial period, that led ultimately to the [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages").

During the Classical period, informal language was rarely written, so philologists have been left with only individual words and phrases cited by classical authors, inscriptions such as [Curse tablets](/wiki/Curse_tablets "Curse tablets") and those found as [graffiti](/wiki/Roman_graffiti "Roman graffiti"). In the [Late Latin](/wiki/Late_Latin "Late Latin") period, language changes reflecting spoken (non-classical) norms tend to be found in greater quantities in texts.[[11]](#cite_note-12)
As it was free to develop on its own, there is no reason to suppose that the speech was uniform either diachronically or geographically. On the contrary, Romanised European populations developed their own [dialects](/wiki/Dialects_of_Latin "Dialects of Latin") of the language, which eventually led to the differentiation of [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages").[[12]](#cite_note-13)

### Late Latin

Main article: [Late Latin](/wiki/Late_Latin "Late Latin")

Late Latin is a kind of written Latin used in the 3rd to 6th centuries. This began to diverge from Classical forms at a faster pace. It is characterised by greater use of prepositions, and word order that is closer to modern Romance languages, for example, while grammatically retaining more or less the same formal rules as Classical Latin.

Ultimately, Latin diverged into a distinct written form, where the commonly spoken form was perceived as a separate language, for instance early French or Italian dialects, that could be transcribed differently. It took some time for these to be viewed as wholly different from Latin however.

After the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") fell in 476 and [Germanic kingdoms](/wiki/Barbarian_kingdoms "Barbarian kingdoms") took its place, the [Germanic people](/wiki/Germanic_people "Germanic people") adopted Latin as a language more suitable for legal and other, more formal uses.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Initially Latin was also retained by the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") (Byzantine Empire) as the language of the government and legislation, even though the vast majority of its population spoke [Greek](/wiki/Medieval_Greek "Medieval Greek") and other Eastern languages such as [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") and [Coptic](/wiki/Coptic_language "Coptic language"). It even enjoyed a brief flowering as the language of the great codification of laws, the [Corpus Iuris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Iuris_Civilis "Corpus Iuris Civilis") under [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I"), himself a native Latin speaker[[14]](#cite_note-15). After Justinian I's death, the gradual territorial retreat of the Empire and its [near collapse](/wiki/Twenty_Years%27_Anarchy "Twenty Years' Anarchy") in the wake of the [Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests") led to the near extinction of Latin as a spoken and official language. The surviving rump Roman state replaced Latin with the Greek language. Latin was retained on coinage and in some court ceremonies[[15]](#cite_note-16) until the [11th century](/wiki/11th_century "11th century"), albeit often in a ritual and fossilised form. In a surviving letter from the late 9th century, the Carolingian emperor [Louis II](/wiki/Louis_II_of_Italy "Louis II of Italy") invoked the fact that the Byzantine imperial chancery struggled to write in proper Latin as an argument in his [ideological dispute over who was the rightful Roman emperor](/wiki/Problem_of_two_emperors "Problem of two emperors").[[16]](#cite_note-17)

### Romance languages

Main article: [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages")

See also: [Lexical changes from Classical Latin to Proto-Romance](/wiki/Lexical_changes_from_Classical_Latin_to_Proto-Romance "Lexical changes from Classical Latin to Proto-Romance")

While the written form of Latin was increasingly standardised into a fixed form, the spoken forms began to diverge more greatly. Currently, the six most widely spoken Romance languages by number of native speakers are [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") and [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"). Despite dialectal variation, which is found in any widespread language, the languages currently existing in Spain, France, Portugal, and Italy have retained a remarkable unity in phonological forms and developments, bolstered by the stabilising influence of their common [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians") ([Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")) culture.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Michael_III_and_Basil_I_sb1693.jpg/330px-Michael_III_and_Basil_I_sb1693.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_III_and_Basil_I_sb1693.jpg)

A 9th century [Byzantine coin](/wiki/Byzantine_coinage "Byzantine coinage") showing emperor [Michael III](/wiki/Michael_III "Michael III") and his then-junior colleague [Basil I](/wiki/Basil_I "Basil I") with the Latin titles "[imperator](/wiki/Imperator "Imperator")" and "[rex](/wiki/Rex_(title) "Rex (title)")", respectively. Although spoken Latin had largely disappeared in the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") by that time, it was occasionally still used on coinage and in ceremonies until at least the 11th century.

It was not until the [Muslim conquest of the Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Iberian_Peninsula "Muslim conquest of the Iberian Peninsula") in 711, cutting off communications between the major Romance regions, that the languages began to diverge seriously.[[17]](#cite_note-18) The spoken Latin that would later become Romanian diverged somewhat more from the other varieties, as it was largely separated from the unifying influences in the western part of the Empire.

Spoken Latin began to diverge into distinct languages by the 9th century at the latest, when the earliest extant Romance writings begin to appear. They were, throughout the period, confined to everyday speech, as Medieval Latin was used for writing.[[18]](#cite_note-:0-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)

For many Italians using Latin, though, there was no complete separation between Italian and Latin, even into the beginning of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") for example saw Latin as a literary version of the spoken language.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### Medieval Latin

Main article: [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Calligraphy.malmesbury.bible.arp.jpg/330px-Calligraphy.malmesbury.bible.arp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calligraphy.malmesbury.bible.arp.jpg)

The Latin Malmesbury Bible, from 1407

Medieval Latin is the written Latin in use during that portion of the post-classical period when no corresponding Latin [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") existed, that is from around 700 to 1500 AD. The spoken language had developed into the various Romance languages; however, in the educated and official world, Latin continued without its natural spoken base. Moreover, this Latin spread into lands that had never spoken Latin, such as the Germanic and Slavic nations. It became useful for international communication between the member states of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and its allies.

Without the institutions of the Roman Empire that had supported its uniformity, Medieval Latin was much more liberal in its linguistic cohesion: for example, in classical Latin *sum* and *eram* are used as auxiliary verbs in the perfect and pluperfect passive, which are compound tenses. Medieval Latin might use *fui* and *fueram* instead.[[21]](#cite_note-thorley13-15-22) Furthermore, the meanings of many words were changed and new words were introduced, often under influence from the vernacular. Identifiable individual styles of classically incorrect Latin prevail.[[21]](#cite_note-thorley13-15-22)

### Renaissance and Neo-Latin

Main articles: [Renaissance Latin](/wiki/Renaissance_Latin "Renaissance Latin") and [Neo-Latin](/wiki/Neo-Latin "Neo-Latin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Incunabula_distribution_by_language.png/250px-Incunabula_distribution_by_language.png)](/wiki/File:Incunabula_distribution_by_language.png)

Most 15th-century printed books ([incunabula](/wiki/Incunabula "Incunabula")) were in Latin, with the [vernacular languages](/wiki/Vernacular_language "Vernacular language") playing only a secondary role.[[22]](#cite_note-ISTC-23)

Renaissance Latin, in use from around 1300 to 1500, and the classicised Latin that followed through to the present are often grouped together as *Neo-Latin*, or New Latin, which have in recent decades become a focus of [renewed study](/wiki/Neo-Latin_studies "Neo-Latin studies"), given their importance for the development of European culture, religion and science.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25) The vast majority of written Latin belongs to this period, but its full extent is unknown.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

The [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") reinforced the position of Latin as a spoken and written language by the scholarship by the [Renaissance humanists](/wiki/Renaissance_Humanism "Renaissance Humanism"). [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") and others began to change their usage of Latin as they explored the texts of the Classical Latin world. Skills of textual criticism evolved to create much more accurate versions of extant texts through the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, and some important texts were rediscovered. Comprehensive versions of authors' works were published by [Isaac Casaubon](/wiki/Isaac_Casaubon "Isaac Casaubon"), [Joseph Scaliger](/wiki/Joseph_Scaliger "Joseph Scaliger") and others.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Nevertheless, despite the careful work of Petrarch, [Politian](/wiki/Politian "Politian") and others, first the demand for manuscripts, and then the rush to bring works into print, led to the circulation of inaccurate copies for several centuries following.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

Neo-Latin literature was extensive and prolific, but less well known or understood today. Works covered poetry, prose stories and early novels, occasional pieces and collections of letters, to name a few. Famous and well regarded writers included Petrarch, [Erasmus](/wiki/Erasmus "Erasmus"), [Salutati](/wiki/Salutati "Salutati"), [Celtis](/wiki/Conrad_Celtes "Conrad Celtes"), [George Buchanan](/wiki/George_Buchanan "George Buchanan") and [Thomas More](/wiki/Thomas_More "Thomas More").[[28]](#cite_note-29) Non fiction works were long produced in many subjects, including the sciences, law, philosophy, historiography and theology. Famous examples include [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton")'s *[Principia](/wiki/Philosophi%C3%A6_Naturalis_Principia_Mathematica "Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica")*. Latin was also used as a convenient medium for translations of important works first written in a vernacular, such as those of [Descartes](/wiki/Descartes "Descartes").

Latin education underwent a process of reform to classicise written and spoken Latin. Schooling was conducted largely in Latin until approximately 1700. Until the end of the 17th century, the majority of books and almost all diplomatic documents were written in Latin.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Afterwards, most diplomatic documents were written in French (a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language")) and later native or other languages.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Education methods gradually shifted towards written Latin, and eventually concentrating solely on reading skills. The decline of Latin education took several centuries and proceeded much more slowly than the decline in written Latin output.

### Contemporary Latin

Main articles: [Contemporary Latin](/wiki/Contemporary_Latin "Contemporary Latin") and [Ecclesiastical Latin](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Latin "Ecclesiastical Latin")

Despite having no native speakers, Latin is still used for a variety of purposes in the contemporary world.

#### Religious use

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Wallsend_platfom_2_02.jpg/250px-Wallsend_platfom_2_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wallsend_platfom_2_02.jpg)

The signs at [Wallsend Metro station](/wiki/Wallsend_Metro_station "Wallsend Metro station") are in English and Latin, as a tribute to [Wallsend](/wiki/Wallsend "Wallsend")'s role as one of the outposts of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), as the eastern end of [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall") (hence the name) at [Segedunum](/wiki/Segedunum "Segedunum").

The largest organisation that retains Latin in official and quasi-official contexts is the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). The Catholic Church required that Mass be carried out in Latin until the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council") of 1962–1965, which permitted the use of the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular_language "Vernacular language"). Latin remains the language of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"). The [Tridentine Mass](/wiki/Tridentine_Mass "Tridentine Mass") (also known as the Extraordinary Form or Traditional Latin Mass) is celebrated in Latin. Although the [Mass of Paul VI](/wiki/Mass_of_Paul_VI "Mass of Paul VI") (also known as the Ordinary Form or the Novus Ordo) is usually celebrated in the local vernacular language, it can be and often is said in Latin, in part or in whole, especially at multilingual gatherings. It is the official language of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), the primary language of its [public journal](/wiki/Public_journal "Public journal"), the *[Acta Apostolicae Sedis](/wiki/Acta_Apostolicae_Sedis "Acta Apostolicae Sedis")*, and the working language of the [Roman Rota](/wiki/Roman_Rota "Roman Rota"). [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") is also home to the world's only [automatic teller machine](/wiki/Automatic_teller_machine "Automatic teller machine") that gives instructions in Latin.[[31]](#cite_note-32) In the [pontifical universities](/wiki/Pontifical_university "Pontifical university") postgraduate courses of [Canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law") are taught in Latin, and papers are written in the same language.

There are a small number of Latin services held in the Anglican church. These include an annual service in Oxford, delivered with a Latin sermon; a relic from the period when Latin was the normal spoken language of the university.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Former_logo_of_the_European_Council_and_Council_of_the_European_Union_%282009%29.svg/250px-Former_logo_of_the_European_Council_and_Council_of_the_European_Union_%282009%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Former_logo_of_the_European_Council_and_Council_of_the_European_Union_(2009).svg)

The polyglot [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") has adopted Latin names in the logos of some of its institutions for the sake of linguistic compromise, an "ecumenical nationalism" common to most of the continent and as a sign of the continent's heritage (such as the [EU Council](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union"): *Consilium*).

#### Use of Latin for mottos

In the Western world, many organisations, governments and schools use Latin for their mottos due to its association with formality, tradition, and the roots of [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture").[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Canada's motto *[A mari usque ad mare](/wiki/A_mari_usque_ad_mare "A mari usque ad mare")* ("from sea to sea") and most [provincial mottos](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_provincial_and_territorial_symbols "List of Canadian provincial and territorial symbols") are also in Latin. The [Canadian Victoria Cross](/wiki/Victoria_Cross_(Canada) "Victoria Cross (Canada)") is modelled after the British [Victoria Cross](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross") which has the inscription "For Valour". Because Canada is officially bilingual, the Canadian medal has replaced the English inscription with the Latin *Pro Valore*.

Spain's motto *[Plus ultra](/wiki/Plus_ultra "Plus ultra")* 'even further', or figuratively "Further!", is also Latin in origin.[[34]](#cite_note-35) It is taken from the personal motto of [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor"), Holy Roman Emperor and King of Spain (as Charles I), and is a reversal of the original phrase *Non terrae plus ultra* ("No land further beyond", "No further!"). According to legend, this phrase was inscribed as a warning on the [Pillars of Hercules](/wiki/Pillars_of_Hercules "Pillars of Hercules"), the rocks on both sides of the [Strait of Gibraltar](/wiki/Strait_of_Gibraltar "Strait of Gibraltar") and the western end of the known Mediterranean world. Charles adopted the motto following the discovery of the New World by Columbus, and it also has metaphorical suggestions of taking risks and striving for excellence.

In the United States the unofficial national motto until 1956 was *[E pluribus unum](/wiki/E_pluribus_unum "E pluribus unum")* meaning "Out of many, one". The motto continues to be featured on the [Great Seal](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_the_United_States "Great Seal of the United States"). It also appears on the flags and seals of both houses of congress and the flags of the states of Michigan, North Dakota, New York, and Wisconsin. The motto's 13 letters symbolically represent the original Thirteen Colonies which revolted from the British Crown. The motto is featured on all presently minted coinage and has been featured in most coinage throughout the nation's history.

Several states of the United States [have Latin mottos](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_and_territory_mottos "List of U.S. state and territory mottos"), such as:

* [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona")'s *Ditat deus* ("God enriches");
* [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut")'s *Qui transtulit sustinet* ("He who transplanted sustains");
* [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas")'s *[Ad astra per aspera](/wiki/Per_aspera_ad_astra "Per aspera ad astra")* ("Through hardships, to the stars");
* [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado")'s *Nil sine numine* ("Nothing without providence");
* [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho")'s *[Esto perpetua](/wiki/Esto_perpetua "Esto perpetua")* ("Let it be perpetual");
* [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan")'s *Si quaeris peninsulam amoenam, circumspice* ("If you seek a pleasant peninsula, look about you"), is based on that of Sir [Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren"), in [St. Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Cathedral "St. Paul's Cathedral");
* [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri")'s *[Salus populi suprema lex esto](/wiki/Salus_populi_suprema_lex_esto "Salus populi suprema lex esto")* ("The health of the people should be the highest law");
* [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)")'s *[Excelsior](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_New_York "Coat of arms of New York")* ("Ever upward");
* [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina")'s *[Esse Quam Videri](/wiki/Esse_Quam_Videri "Esse Quam Videri")* ("To be rather than to seem");
* [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina")'s *[Dum spiro spero](/wiki/Dum_spiro_spero "Dum spiro spero")* ("While I breathe, I hope");
* [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia")'s *[Sic semper tyrannis](/wiki/Sic_semper_tyrannis "Sic semper tyrannis")* ("Thus always to [tyrants](/wiki/Tyrant "Tyrant")"); and
* [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia")'s *[Montani Semper Liberi](/wiki/Montani_Semper_Liberi "Montani Semper Liberi")* ("Mountaineers [are] always free").

Many military organisations today have Latin mottos, such as:

* *[Semper Paratus](/wiki/Semper_Paratus "Semper Paratus")* ("always ready"), the motto of the [United States Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard");
* *[Semper Fidelis](/wiki/Semper_Fidelis "Semper Fidelis")* ("always faithful"), the motto of the [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps");
* [*Semper Supra*](/wiki/Semper_supra "Semper supra") ("always above"), the motto of the [United States Space Force](/wiki/United_States_Space_Force "United States Space Force");
* *[Per ardua ad astra](/wiki/Per_ardua_ad_astra "Per ardua ad astra")* ("Through adversity/struggle to the stars"), the motto of the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") (RAF); and
* *Vigilamus pro te* ("We stand on guard for thee"), the motto of the [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces").

Some law governing bodies in the Philippines have Latin mottos, such as:

* *Justitiae Pax Opus* ("The work of Justice is Peace"), the motto of the [Department of Justice (Philippines)](/wiki/Department_of_Justice_(Philippines) "Department of Justice (Philippines)");

Some colleges and universities have adopted Latin mottos, for example [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University")'s motto is *[Veritas](/wiki/Veritas "Veritas")* ("truth"). Veritas was the goddess of truth, a daughter of Saturn, and the mother of Virtue.

#### Other modern uses

Switzerland has adopted the country's Latin short name *[Helvetia](/wiki/Helvetia "Helvetia")* on coins and stamps, since there is no room to use all of the nation's [four official languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Switzerland "Languages of Switzerland"). For a similar reason, it adopted the international vehicle code *CH* and the Internet top-level domain *ch*, for *Confoederatio Helvetica*, the country's full Latin name.

Some film and television in ancient settings, such as *[Sebastiane](/wiki/Sebastiane "Sebastiane")*, *[The Passion of the Christ](/wiki/The_Passion_of_the_Christ "The Passion of the Christ")* and *[Barbarians (2020 TV series)](/wiki/Barbarians_(2020_TV_series) "Barbarians (2020 TV series)")*, have been made with dialogue in Latin. Occasionally, Latin dialogue is used because of its association with religion or philosophy, in such film/television series as *[The Exorcist](/wiki/The_Exorcist "The Exorcist")* and *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(TV_series) "Lost (TV series)")* ("[Jughead](/wiki/Jughead_(Lost) "Jughead (Lost)")"). Subtitles are usually shown for the benefit of those who do not understand Latin. There are also [songs written with Latin lyrics](/wiki/List_of_songs_with_Latin_lyrics "List of songs with Latin lyrics"). The libretto for the opera-oratorio *[Oedipus rex](/wiki/Oedipus_rex_(opera) "Oedipus rex (opera)")* by [Igor Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky") is in Latin.

The continued instruction of Latin is seen by some as a highly valuable component of a liberal arts education. Latin is taught at many high schools, especially in Europe and the Americas. It is most common in British [public schools](/wiki/Public_school_(United_Kingdom) "Public school (United Kingdom)") and grammar schools, the Italian *[liceo classico](/wiki/Liceo_classico "Liceo classico")* and *[liceo scientifico](/wiki/Liceo_scientifico "Liceo scientifico")*, the German *Humanistisches [Gymnasium](/wiki/Gymnasium_(Germany) "Gymnasium (Germany)")* and the Dutch *[gymnasium](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)")*.

QDP Ep 84 – De Ludo "Mysterium": A Latin-language podcast

Occasionally, some media outlets, targeting enthusiasts, broadcast in Latin. Notable examples include [Radio Bremen](/wiki/Radio_Bremen "Radio Bremen") in Germany, [YLE](/wiki/YLE "YLE") radio in Finland (the [Nuntii Latini](/wiki/Nuntii_Latini "Nuntii Latini") broadcast from 1989 until it was shut down in June 2019),[[35]](#cite_note-RTE2019-06-24a-36) and Vatican Radio & Television, all of which broadcast news segments and other material in Latin.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)

A variety of organisations, as well as informal Latin *circuli*, 'circles', have been founded in more recent times to support the use of spoken Latin.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Moreover, a number of university classics departments have begun incorporating communicative pedagogies in their Latin courses. These include the University of Kentucky, the University of Oxford and Princeton University.[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42)

There are many websites and forums maintained in Latin by enthusiasts. The [Latin Wikipedia](/wiki/Latin_Wikipedia "Latin Wikipedia") has more than 140,000 articles.

## Legacy

[Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), [Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language"), [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language") and other [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") are direct descendants of Latin. There are also many Latin [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") in English and [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"),[[42]](#cite_note-Sawicka-43) as well as a few in [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"), [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language") and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language").[[43]](#cite_note-44) Latin is still spoken in Vatican City, a city-state situated in Rome that is the seat of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Commentarii_de_Bello_Gallico.jpg/500px-Commentarii_de_Bello_Gallico.jpg)](/wiki/File:Commentarii_de_Bello_Gallico.jpg)

[Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar")'s *[Commentarii de Bello Gallico](/wiki/Commentarii_de_Bello_Gallico "Commentarii de Bello Gallico")* is one of the most famous classical Latin texts of the Golden Age of Latin. The unvarnished, journalistic style of this [patrician](/wiki/Patrician_(ancient_Rome) "Patrician (ancient Rome)") general has long been taught as a model of the urbane Latin officially spoken and written in the [floruit](/wiki/Floruit "Floruit") of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic").

The works of several hundred ancient authors who wrote in Latin have survived in whole or in part, in substantial works or in fragments to be analysed in [philology](/wiki/Philology "Philology"). They are in part the subject matter of the field of [classics](/wiki/Classics "Classics"). Their works were published in manuscript form before the invention of printing and are now published in carefully annotated printed editions, such as the [Loeb Classical Library](/wiki/Loeb_Classical_Library "Loeb Classical Library"), published by [Harvard University Press](/wiki/Harvard_University_Press "Harvard University Press"), or the [Oxford Classical Texts](/wiki/Oxford_Classical_Texts "Oxford Classical Texts"), published by [Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Latin translations of modern literature](/wiki/Latin_translations_of_modern_literature "Latin translations of modern literature") such as: *[The Hobbit](/wiki/The_Hobbit "The Hobbit")*, *[Treasure Island](/wiki/Treasure_Island "Treasure Island")*, *[Robinson Crusoe](/wiki/Robinson_Crusoe "Robinson Crusoe")*, *[Paddington Bear](/wiki/Paddington_Bear "Paddington Bear")*, *[Winnie the Pooh](/wiki/Winnie_the_Pooh "Winnie the Pooh")*, *[The Adventures of Tintin](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Tintin "The Adventures of Tintin")*, *[Asterix](/wiki/Asterix "Asterix")*, *[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter")*, *[Le Petit Prince](/wiki/The_Little_Prince "The Little Prince")*, *[Max and Moritz](/wiki/Max_and_Moritz "Max and Moritz")*, *[How the Grinch Stole Christmas!](/wiki/How_the_Grinch_Stole_Christmas! "How the Grinch Stole Christmas!")*, *[The Cat in the Hat](/wiki/The_Cat_in_the_Hat "The Cat in the Hat")*, and a book of fairy tales, *fabulae mirabiles*, are intended to garner popular interest in the language. Additional resources include phrasebooks and resources for rendering everyday phrases and concepts into Latin, such as [Meissner's Latin Phrasebook](/wiki/Meissner%27s_Latin_Phrasebook "Meissner's Latin Phrasebook").

### Inscriptions

Some inscriptions have been published in an internationally agreed, monumental, multivolume series, the *[Corpus Inscriptionum Latinarum](/wiki/Corpus_Inscriptionum_Latinarum "Corpus Inscriptionum Latinarum")*. Authors and publishers vary, but the format is about the same: volumes detailing inscriptions with a critical apparatus stating the [provenance](/wiki/Provenance "Provenance") and relevant information. The reading and interpretation of these inscriptions is the subject matter of the field of [epigraphy](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy"). About 270,000 inscriptions are known.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

### Influence on present-day languages

The [Latin influence in English](/wiki/Latin_influence_in_English "Latin influence in English") has been significant at all stages of its insular development. In the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), borrowing from Latin occurred from ecclesiastical usage established by Saint [Augustine of Canterbury](/wiki/Augustine_of_Canterbury "Augustine of Canterbury") in the 6th century or indirectly after the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), through the [Anglo-Norman language](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] From the 16th to the 18th centuries, English writers cobbled together huge numbers of new words from Latin and Greek words, dubbed "[inkhorn terms](/wiki/Inkhorn_term "Inkhorn term")", as if they had spilled from a pot of ink. Many of these words were used once by the author and then forgotten, but some useful ones survived, such as *imbibe* and *extrapolate*. Many of the most common [polysyllabic](/wiki/Polysyllabic "Polysyllabic") English words are of Latin origin through the medium of [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French"). Romance words make up 59%, 20% and 14% of English, German and Dutch vocabularies, respectively.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-Walter-48) Those figures can rise dramatically when only non-compound and non-derived words are included.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Romance_20c_en_%28cropped%29.png/250px-Romance_20c_en_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:Romance_20c_en_(cropped).png)

Range of the Romance languages, the modern descendants of Latin, in Europe

The influence of Roman governance and [Roman technology](/wiki/Roman_technology "Roman technology") on the less-developed nations under Roman dominion led to the adoption of Latin phraseology in some specialised areas, such as science, technology, medicine, and law. For example, [the Linnaean system](/wiki/Linnaean_taxonomy "Linnaean taxonomy") of plant and animal classification was heavily influenced by *[Historia Naturalis](/wiki/Natural_History_(Pliny) "Natural History (Pliny)")*, an encyclopaedia of people, places, plants, animals, and things published by [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder"). Roman medicine, recorded in the works of such physicians as [Galen](/wiki/Galen "Galen"), established that today's [medical terminology](/wiki/Medical_terminology "Medical terminology") would be primarily derived from Latin and Greek words, the Greek being filtered through the Latin. Roman engineering had the same effect on [scientific terminology](/wiki/Scientific_terminology "Scientific terminology") as a whole. Latin law principles have survived partly in a long [list of Latin legal terms](/wiki/List_of_Latin_legal_terms "List of Latin legal terms").

The [Logudorese](/wiki/Logudorese "Logudorese") dialect of the [Sardinian language](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language") and [Standard Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") are the two closest contemporary languages to Latin.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

### Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Latin_dictionary.jpg/250px-Latin_dictionary.jpg)](/wiki/File:Latin_dictionary.jpg)

A multivolume Latin dictionary in the [University of Graz Library](/wiki/University_of_Graz_Library "University of Graz Library") in Austria

Throughout European history, an education in the classics was considered crucial for those who wished to join literate circles. The prominence of Latin in classical education rested not only on tradition but also on its reputation for clarity, logical structure, and intellectual rigor, a view expressed by the mathematics educator [Theodor Haagaas](/wiki/Theodor_Haagaas "Theodor Haagaas"), who remarked that "Latin, it is mathematics, language mathematics."[[49]](#cite_note-50)

This also was true in the United States where many of the nation's founders obtained a classically based education in grammar schools or from tutors.[[50]](#cite_note-51) Admission to Harvard in the Colonial era required that the applicant "Can readily make and speak or write true Latin prose and has skill in making verse"[[51]](#cite_note-52) Latin Study and the classics were emphasised in American secondary schools and colleges well into the Antebellum era.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

[Instruction in Latin](/wiki/Instruction_in_Latin "Instruction in Latin") is an essential aspect. In today's world, a large number of Latin students in the United States learn from *Wheelock's Latin: The Classic Introductory Latin Course, Based on Ancient Authors*. This book, first published in 1956,[[53]](#cite_note-54) was written by [Frederic M. Wheelock](/wiki/Frederic_M._Wheelock "Frederic M. Wheelock"). *Wheelock's Latin* has become the standard text for many American introductory Latin courses.

The numbers of people studying Latin varies significantly by country. In the United Kingdom, Latin is available in around 2.3% of state primary schools, representing a significant increase in availability.[[54]](#cite_note-55) In Germany, over 500,000 students study Latin each year, representing a decrease from over 800,000 in 2008. Latin is still required for some University courses, but this has become less frequent.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

The [Living Latin](/wiki/Living_Latin "Living Latin") movement attempts to teach Latin in the same way that living languages are taught, as a means of both spoken and written communication. It is available in Vatican City and at some institutions in the US, such as the [University of Kentucky](/wiki/University_of_Kentucky "University of Kentucky") and [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University"). The British [Cambridge University Press](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press "Cambridge University Press") is a major supplier of Latin textbooks for all levels, such as the [Cambridge Latin Course](/wiki/Cambridge_Latin_Course "Cambridge Latin Course") series. It has also published a subseries of children's texts in Latin by Bell & Forte, which recounts the adventures of a mouse called [Minimus](/wiki/Minimus "Minimus").

In the United Kingdom, the [Classical Association](/wiki/Classical_Association "Classical Association") encourages the study of antiquity through various means, such as publications and grants. The [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"),[[56]](#cite_note-57) the [Open University](/wiki/Open_University "Open University"),[[57]](#cite_note-58) a number of independent schools, for example [Eton](/wiki/Eton_College "Eton College"), [Harrow](/wiki/Harrow_School "Harrow School"), [Haberdashers' Aske's Boys' School](/wiki/Haberdashers%27_Aske%27s_Boys%27_School "Haberdashers' Aske's Boys' School"), [Merchant Taylors' School](/wiki/Merchant_Taylors%27_School,_Northwood "Merchant Taylors' School, Northwood"), and [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School"), and The Latin Programme/Via Facilis,[[58]](#cite_note-59) a London-based charity, run Latin courses. In the United States and in Canada, the [American Classical League](/wiki/American_Classical_League "American Classical League") supports every effort to further the study of classics. Its subsidiaries include the [National Junior Classical League](/wiki/National_Junior_Classical_League "National Junior Classical League") (with more than 50,000 members), which encourages high school students to pursue the study of Latin, and the [National Senior Classical League](/wiki/National_Senior_Classical_League "National Senior Classical League"), which encourages students to continue their study of the classics into college. The league also sponsors the [National Latin Exam](/wiki/National_Latin_Exam "National Latin Exam"). Classicist [Mary Beard](/wiki/Mary_Beard_(classicist) "Mary Beard (classicist)") wrote in *[The Times Literary Supplement](/wiki/The_Times_Literary_Supplement "The Times Literary Supplement")* in 2006 that the reason for learning Latin is because of what was written in it.[[59]](#cite_note-timesonline_train_the_brain-60)

### Official status

Latin was or is the official language of several European states. It had official status in the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") from the 11th to mid-19th centuries, when [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") became the exclusive official language in 1844.[[60]](#cite_note-61) The best known Latin language poet of Hungarian origin was [Janus Pannonius](/wiki/Janus_Pannonius "Janus Pannonius"). Similarly, in the [Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland "Kingdom of Poland") and the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), Latin was officially recognised and widely used[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-Friedrich-65) between the 10th and 18th centuries, commonly used in foreign relations and popular as a second language among some of the [nobility](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta").[[64]](#cite_note-Friedrich-65) Latin was also the official language of the [Croatian Parliament](/wiki/Croatian_Parliament "Croatian Parliament") from the 13th to the 19th century (1847).[[65]](#cite_note-66) The oldest preserved records of the parliamentary sessions (*Congregatio Regni totius Sclavonie generalis*)—held in Zagreb (*Zagabria*), Croatia—date from 19 April 1273. An extensive [Croatian Latin literature](/wiki/Croatian_Latin_literature "Croatian Latin literature") exists. Latin was used on Croatian coins on even years until 1 January 2023, when Croatia adopted the Euro as its official currency.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

## Phonology

Main article: [Latin phonology and orthography](/wiki/Latin_phonology_and_orthography "Latin phonology and orthography")

Audio of a person with a German accent reading in Latin



[Ave Maria](/wiki/Ave_Maria "Ave Maria") read in Ecclesiastical Latin

The ancient pronunciation of Latin has been reconstructed; among the data used for reconstruction are explicit statements about pronunciation by ancient authors, misspellings, puns, ancient etymologies, the spelling of Latin loanwords in other languages, and the historical development of Romance languages.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

### Consonants

/z/ was not native to Classical Latin. It appeared in Greek loanwords starting c. the 1st century BC, when it was probably pronounced (at least by educated speakers) [z] initially and [doubled](/wiki/Gemination "Gemination") [zz] between vowels, in accordance with its pronunciation in [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek"). In Classical Latin poetry, the letter ⟨z⟩ between vowels always counts as two consonants for metrical purposes.[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-Allen_Z-71) The consonant ⟨b⟩ usually sounds as [b]; however, when ⟨t⟩ or ⟨s⟩ follows ⟨b⟩ then it is pronounced as in [pt] or [ps]. In Latin, ⟨q⟩ is always followed by the [vowel](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") ⟨u⟩. Together they make a [kʷ] sound.[[71]](#cite_note-Wheelock_7th_ed.-72)

In Old and Classical Latin, the Latin alphabet had no distinction between [uppercase and lowercase](/wiki/Letter_case "Letter case"), and the letters ⟨J U W⟩ did not exist. In place of ⟨J U⟩, ⟨I V⟩ were used, respectively; ⟨I V⟩ represented both vowels and consonants. Most of the letter forms were similar to modern uppercase, as can be seen in the inscription from the Colosseum shown at the top of the article.

The spelling systems used in Latin dictionaries and modern editions of Latin texts, however, normally use ⟨j u⟩ in place of Classical-era ⟨i v⟩. Some systems use ⟨j v⟩ for the consonant sounds /j w/ except in the combinations ⟨gu su qu⟩ for which ⟨v⟩ is never used.

In Classical Latin, as in modern Italian, double consonant letters were pronounced as [long](/wiki/Gemination "Gemination") consonant sounds distinct from short versions of the same consonants. Thus the *nn* in Classical Latin *annus* 'year' (and in Italian *anno*) is pronounced as a doubled /nn/ as in English *unnamed*. (In English, distinctive consonant length or doubling occurs only at the boundary between two words or [morphemes](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme"), as in that example.)

### Vowels

Simple vowels[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)

|  | Front | Central | Back |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Close | [i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") [iː](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") |  | [u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") [uː](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") |
| Mid | [ɛ](/wiki/Open-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Open-mid front unrounded vowel") [ɛː](/wiki/Open-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Open-mid front unrounded vowel") |  | [ɔ](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel") [ɔː](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel") |
| Open |  | [a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") [aː](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") |  |

In Classical Latin, ⟨U⟩ did not exist as a letter distinct from ⟨V⟩; the written form ⟨V⟩ was used to represent both a vowel and a consonant. ⟨Y⟩ was adopted to represent [upsilon](/wiki/Upsilon "Upsilon") in loanwords from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), but it was pronounced like ⟨u⟩ and ⟨i⟩ by some speakers. It was also used in native Latin words by confusion with Greek words of similar meaning, such as *sylva* and ὕλη *hū́lē*.

Classical Latin distinguished between [long and short vowels](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length"). Then, long vowels, except for ⟨i⟩, were frequently marked using the [apex](/wiki/Apex_(diacritic) "Apex (diacritic)"), which was sometimes similar to an [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent") ⟨Á É Ó V́ Ý⟩. Long /iː/ was written using a taller version of ⟨I⟩, called *i longa* '[long *I*](/wiki/Long_I "Long I")': ⟨ꟾ⟩. In modern texts, long vowels are often indicated by a [macron](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)") ⟨ā ē ī ō ū⟩, and short vowels are usually unmarked except when it is necessary to distinguish between words, when they are marked with a [breve](/wiki/Breve "Breve") ⟨ă ĕ ĭ ŏ ŭ⟩. However, they would also signify a long vowel by writing the vowel larger than other letters in a word or by repeating the vowel twice in a row.[[71]](#cite_note-Wheelock_7th_ed.-72) The acute accent, when it is used in modern Latin texts, indicates stress, as in Spanish, rather than length.

Although called long vowels, their exact quality in Classical Latin is different from short vowels. The difference is described in the table below:

Pronunciation of Latin vowels

| Grapheme | Phone | Modern examples |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ⟨a⟩ | [a] | Similar to the *a* in *part* (/paɹt/) |
| [aː] | Similar to the *a* in *father* (/fɑːðəɹ/) |
| ⟨e⟩ | [ɛ] | As *e* in *pet* (/pɛt/) |
| [ɛː] | ɛ but longer |
| ⟨i⟩ | [i] | iː but shorter |
| [iː] | Similar to *i* in *machine* (/məʃiːn/) |
| ⟨o⟩ | [ɔ] | As *o* in *port* (/pɔɹt/) |
| [ɔː] | ɔ but longer |
| ⟨u⟩ | [u] | uː but shorter |
| [uː] | Similar to *ue* in *true* (/tɹuː/) |
| ⟨y⟩ | [ʏ] | Does not exist in English, closest approximation is the *u* in *mule* |
| [ʏː] | ʏ but longer. |

This difference in quality is posited by [W. Sidney Allen](/wiki/W._Sidney_Allen "W. Sidney Allen") in his book *Vox Latina*. However, [Andrea Calabrese](/w/index.php?title=Andrea_Calabrese&action=edit&redlink=1 "Andrea Calabrese (page does not exist)") has disputed this assertion, based in part upon the observation that in Sardinian and some Lucanian dialects, each long and short vowel pair merged, as opposed to in Italo-Western languages in which short /i/ and /u/ merged with long /eː/ and /o:/ (cf. Latin *siccus*, Italian *secco*, and Sardinian *siccu*).[[76]](#cite_note-77)

A vowel letter followed by ⟨m⟩ at the end of a word, or a vowel letter followed by ⟨n⟩ before ⟨s⟩ or ⟨f⟩, represented a short [nasal vowel](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel"), as in *monstrum* [mõːstrũ].

#### Diphthongs

Classical Latin had several [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"). The two most common were ⟨ae au⟩. The former is pronounced like the *i* in *mine*, and the latter like the *ow* in *power*. ⟨oe⟩ was fairly rare, and ⟨ui eu ei⟩ were very rare, at least in native Latin words.[[77]](#cite_note-classical_diphthongs-78) There has also been debate over whether ⟨ui⟩ is truly a diphthong in Classical Latin, due to its rarity, absence in works of Roman grammarians, and the roots of Classical Latin words (e.g. *hui ce* to *huic*, *quoi* to *cui*) not matching or being similar to the pronunciation of classical words if ⟨ui⟩ were to be considered a diphthong.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The sequences sometimes did not represent diphthongs. ⟨ae⟩ and ⟨oe⟩ also represented a sequence of two vowels in different syllables in *aēnus* [aˈeː.nʊs] 'bronze' and *coēpit* [kɔˈeː.pɪt] 'began', and ⟨au ui eu ei ou⟩ represented sequences of two vowels or of a vowel and one of the semivowels /j w/, in *cavē* [ˈka.weː] 'beware!', *cuius* [ˈkʊj.jʊs] 'whose', *monuī* [ˈmɔn.ʊ.iː] 'I warned', *solvī* [ˈsɔɫ.wiː] 'I released', *dēlēvī* [deːˈleː.wiː] 'I destroyed', *eius* [ˈɛj.jʊs] 'his', and *novus* [ˈnɔ.wʊs] 'new'.

Old Latin had more diphthongs, but most of them changed into long vowels in Classical Latin. The Old Latin diphthong ⟨ai⟩ and the sequence ⟨āī⟩ became Classical ⟨ae⟩. Old Latin ⟨oi⟩ and ⟨ou⟩ changed to Classical ⟨ū⟩, except in a few words whose ⟨oi⟩ became Classical ⟨oe⟩. These two developments sometimes occurred in different words from the same root: for instance, Classical *poena* "punishment" and *pūnīre* "to punish".[[77]](#cite_note-classical_diphthongs-78) Early Old Latin ⟨ei⟩ usually monophthongised to a later Old Latin ⟨ē⟩, to Classical ⟨ī⟩.[[79]](#cite_note-80)

By the late Roman Empire, ⟨ae oe⟩ had merged with ⟨e ē⟩. During the Classical period this sound change was present in some rural dialects, but deliberately avoided by well-educated speakers.[[77]](#cite_note-classical_diphthongs-78)

### Syllables

[Syllables](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") in Latin are signified by the presence of diphthongs and [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel"). The number of syllables is the same as the number of vowel sounds.[[71]](#cite_note-Wheelock_7th_ed.-72)

Further, if a consonant separates two vowels, it will go into the syllable of the second vowel. When there are two consonants between vowels, the last consonant will go with the second vowel. An exception occurs when a [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription") stop and liquid come together. In this situation, they are thought to be a single consonant, and as such, they will go into the syllable of the second vowel.[[71]](#cite_note-Wheelock_7th_ed.-72)

#### Length

Syllables in Latin are considered either [long or short](/wiki/Syllable_weight#Basic_definitions "Syllable weight") (less often called "heavy" and "light" respectively). Within a word, a syllable may either be long by nature or long by position.[[71]](#cite_note-Wheelock_7th_ed.-72) A syllable is long by nature if it has a diphthong or a long vowel. On the other hand, a syllable is long by position if the vowel is followed by more than one consonant.[[71]](#cite_note-Wheelock_7th_ed.-72)

#### Stress

There are two rules that define which syllable is [stressed](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") in Classical Latin.[[71]](#cite_note-Wheelock_7th_ed.-72)

1. In a word with only two syllables, the emphasis will be on the first syllable.
2. In a word with more than two syllables, there are two cases.
   * If the second-to-last syllable is long, that syllable will have stress.
   * If the second-to-last syllable is not long, the syllable before that one will be stressed instead.[[71]](#cite_note-Wheelock_7th_ed.-72)

## Orthography

Main articles: [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") and [Latin phonology and orthography](/wiki/Latin_phonology_and_orthography "Latin phonology and orthography")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Duenos_inscription.jpg/250px-Duenos_inscription.jpg)](/wiki/File:Duenos_inscription.jpg)

The [Duenos Inscription](/wiki/Duenos_Inscription "Duenos Inscription"), from the 6th century BC, is one of the earliest known [Old Latin](/wiki/Old_Latin "Old Latin") texts. It was found on the [Quirinal Hill](/wiki/Quirinal_Hill "Quirinal Hill") in Rome.

Latin was written in the Latin alphabet ([A](/wiki/A "A"), [B](/wiki/B "B"), [C](/wiki/C "C"), [D](/wiki/D "D"), [E](/wiki/E "E"), [F](/wiki/F "F"), [G](/wiki/G "G"), [H](/wiki/H "H"), [I](/wiki/I "I"), [K](/wiki/K "K"), [L](/wiki/L "L"), [M](/wiki/M "M"), [N](/wiki/N "N"), [O](/wiki/O "O"), [P](/wiki/P "P"), [Q](/wiki/Q "Q"), [R](/wiki/R "R"), [S](/wiki/S "S"), [T](/wiki/T "T"), [V](/wiki/V "V"), [X](/wiki/X "X")), derived from the [Etruscan alphabet](/wiki/Etruscan_alphabet "Etruscan alphabet"), which was in turn drawn from the [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet") and ultimately the [Phoenician alphabet](/wiki/Phoenician_alphabet "Phoenician alphabet").[[80]](#cite_note-81) This alphabet has continued to be used over the centuries as the script for the Romance, Celtic, Germanic, Baltic, Finnic and many Slavic languages ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"), [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), [Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"), [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") and [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language")); and it has been adopted by many languages around the world, including [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"), the [Austronesian languages](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages"), many [Turkic languages](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages"), and most languages in [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), the Americas and Oceania, making it by far the world's single most widely used writing system.

The number of letters in the Latin alphabet has varied. When it was first derived from the Etruscan alphabet, it contained only 21 letters.[[81]](#cite_note-82) Later, *G* was added to represent /ɡ/, which had previously been spelled *C*, and *Z* ceased to be included in the alphabet, as the language then had no [voiced alveolar fricative](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_fricative "Voiced alveolar fricative").[[82]](#cite_note-D538-83) The letters K, *Y*, and *Z* were later added to represent Greek letters [kappa](/wiki/Kappa "Kappa"), [upsilon](/wiki/Upsilon "Upsilon"), and [zeta](/wiki/Zeta "Zeta") respectively, in Greek loanwords.[[82]](#cite_note-D538-83)

*W* was created in the 11th century from *VV* in some areas and *UU* in others. It represented /w/ in Germanic languages, not Latin, which still uses *V* for the purpose. *J* was distinguished from the original *I* only during the late Middle Ages, as was the letter *U* from *V*.[[82]](#cite_note-D538-83) Although some Latin dictionaries use *J*, it is rarely used for Latin text, as it was not used in classical times, but many other languages use it.

### Punctuation

Classical Latin did not contain sentence [punctuation](/wiki/Punctuation "Punctuation"), letter case,[[83]](#cite_note-84) or [interword spacing](/wiki/Interword_spacing "Interword spacing"), but [apices](/wiki/Apex_(diacritic) "Apex (diacritic)") were sometimes used to distinguish length in vowels and the [interpunct](/wiki/Interpunct "Interpunct") was used at times to separate words.

The first line of [Catullus 3](/wiki/Catullus_3 "Catullus 3") ("Mourn, O [Venuses](/wiki/Venus_(mythology) "Venus (mythology)") and [Cupids](/wiki/Cupid "Cupid")") was originally written as:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| simply | lv́géteóveneréscupidinésqve |
| with [long I](/wiki/Long_I "Long I") | lv́géteóveneréscupIdinésqve |
| with interpunct | lv́géte·ó·venerés·cupidinésqve |

It would be rendered in a modern edition as:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| simply | *Lugete, o Veneres Cupidinesque* |
| with macrons | *Lūgēte, ō Venerēs Cupīdinēsque* |
| with apices | *Lúgéte, ó Venerés Cupídinésque* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Hocgracili.jpg/330px-Hocgracili.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hocgracili.jpg)

A modern Latin text written in the Old Roman Cursive inspired by the [Vindolanda tablets](/wiki/Vindolanda_tablets "Vindolanda tablets"), the oldest surviving handwritten documents in Britain. The word *Romani* 'Romans' is at bottom left.

The [Roman cursive](/wiki/Roman_cursive "Roman cursive") script is commonly found on the many [wax tablets](/wiki/Wax_tablet "Wax tablet") excavated at sites such as forts, an especially extensive set having been discovered at Vindolanda on [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall") in Britain. Most notable is the fact that while most of the [Vindolanda tablets](/wiki/Vindolanda_tablets "Vindolanda tablets") show spaces between words, spaces were avoided in monumental inscriptions from that era.

## Grammar

Main articles: [Latin grammar](/wiki/Latin_grammar "Latin grammar") and [Latin syntax](/wiki/Latin_syntax "Latin syntax")

[![An educational diagram displaying the syntax of the Latin sentence Iohannes vidit illam puellam with nodes representing John, sees, and girl, aiding in understanding language structure.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Iohannesviditpuellam.jpg/250px-Iohannesviditpuellam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iohannesviditpuellam.jpg)

Syntactical structure of the Latin sentence *Iohannes vidit illam puellam*, meaning 'John sees the girl'

Latin is a [synthetic](/wiki/Synthetic_language "Synthetic language"), [fusional language](/wiki/Fusional_language "Fusional language") in the terminology of linguistic typology. Words involve an objective semantic element and markers (usually suffixes) specifying the grammatical use of the word, expressing [gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"), and [case](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") in [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"), [nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun"), and [pronouns](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun") (*[declension](/wiki/Declension "Declension")*) and verbs to denote [person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person"), [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"), [tense](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense"), [voice](/wiki/Grammatical_voice "Grammatical voice"), [mood](/wiki/Grammatical_mood "Grammatical mood"), and [aspect](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") (*[conjugation](/wiki/Grammatical_conjugation "Grammatical conjugation")*). Some words are uninflected and undergo neither process, such as adverbs, prepositions, and [interjections](/wiki/Latin_interjections "Latin interjections").

Latin inflection can result in words with much ambiguity: For example, *amābit* 'he/she/it will love', is formed from *amā-*, a future tense morpheme *-bi-* and a third person singular morpheme, *-t*, the last of which *-t* does not express masculine, feminine, or neuter gender. A major task in understanding Latin phrases and clauses is to clarify such ambiguities by an analysis of context. [Latin word order](/wiki/Latin_word_order "Latin word order") is relatively free because inflections disambiguate semantic connections, but different word orders can indicate different nuances of meaning.

### Nouns

Main article: [Latin declension](/wiki/Latin_declension "Latin declension")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

All regular Latin nouns and adjectives belong to one of five declensions; a grouping of nouns and adjectives with similar inflected forms. The declensions are identified by final letter of the stem (the word without the ending declension) and the genitive singular ending of the word.

| Declension | Final Letter of the Stem | Genitive Singular Ending |
| --- | --- | --- |
| First | ā | -ae |
| Second | ŏ | -ī |
| Third | ĭ / Some consonant | -īs |
| Fourth | ŭ | -ūs |
| Fifth | ē | -ēī / -ĕī[[84]](#cite_note-85) |

There are seven Latin noun cases, which also apply to adjectives and pronouns and mark a noun's syntactic role in the sentence by means of inflections. Thus, [word order in Latin](/wiki/Latin_word_order "Latin word order") is not as important as it is in English, which is less inflected. The general structure and word order of a Latin sentence can therefore vary. The cases are as follows:

1. [Nominative](/wiki/Nominative_case "Nominative case") – used when the noun is the [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") or a [predicate nominative](/wiki/Predicate_nominative "Predicate nominative"). The thing or person acting: the girl ran: *puella cucurrit*, or *cucurrit puella*
2. [Genitive](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") – used when the noun is the possessor of or connected with an object: "the horse of the man", or "the man's horse"; in both instances, the word *man* would be in the [genitive case](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") when it is translated into Latin. It also indicates the [partitive](/wiki/Partitive "Partitive"), in which the material is quantified: "a group of people"; "a number of gifts": *people* and *gifts* would be in the genitive case. Some nouns are genitive with special verbs and adjectives: The cup is full of wine. (*Pōculum plēnum vīnī est.*) The master of the slave had beaten him. (*Dominus servī eum verberāverat.*)
3. [Dative](/wiki/Dative_case "Dative case") – used when the noun is the indirect object of the sentence, with special verbs, with certain prepositions, and if it is used as agent, reference, or even possessor: The merchant hands the [stola](/wiki/Stola "Stola") to the woman. (*Mercātor fēminae stolam trādit.*)
4. [Accusative](/wiki/Accusative_case "Accusative case") – used when the noun is the direct object of the subject, as the object of a preposition demonstrating place to which, and sometimes to indicate a duration of time: The man killed the boy. (*Vir puerum necāvit.*)
5. [Ablative](/wiki/Ablative_case "Ablative case") – used when the noun demonstrates separation or movement from a source, cause, [agent](/wiki/Agent_(grammar) "Agent (grammar)") or [instrument](/wiki/Instrumental_case "Instrumental case") or when the noun is used as the object of certain prepositions, and to indicate a specific place in time.; adverbial: You walked with the boy. (*Cum puerō ambulāvistī.*)
6. [Vocative](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case") – used when the noun is used in a [direct address](/wiki/Noun_of_address "Noun of address"). The vocative form of a noun is often the same as the nominative, with the exception of second-declension nouns ending in *-us*. The *-us* becomes an *-e* in the vocative singular. If it ends in *-ius* (such as *fīlius*), the ending is just *-ī* (*filī*), as distinct from the nominative plural (*filiī*) in the vocative singular: "Master!" shouted the slave. (*"Domine!" clāmāvit servus.*)
7. [Locative](/wiki/Locative_case "Locative case") – used to indicate a location (corresponding to the English *in* or *at*. It is far less common than the other six cases of Latin nouns and usually applies to cities and small towns and islands along with a few common nouns, such as the words *domus* 'house', *humus* 'ground', and *rus* 'country'. In the singular of the first and second declensions, its form coincides with the genitive (*Roma* becomes *Romae* 'in Rome'. In the plural of all declensions and the singular of the other declensions, it coincides with the ablative (*Athēnae* becomes *Athēnīs*, 'at Athens'. In the fourth-declension word *domus*, the locative form, *domī* 'at home' differs from the standard form of all other cases.

Latin lacks both definite and indefinite [articles](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)"), so *puer currit* can mean either 'the boy is running' or 'a boy is running'.

### Adjectives

Main article: [Latin declension § Adjectives](/wiki/Latin_declension#Adjectives "Latin declension")

There are two types of regular Latin adjectives: first- and second-declension and third-declension. They are so-called because their forms are similar or identical to first- and second-declension and third-declension nouns, respectively. Latin adjectives also have [comparative and superlative](/wiki/Comparative_and_superlative "Comparative and superlative") forms. There are also a number of Latin [participles](/wiki/Participles "Participles").

Latin numbers are sometimes declined as adjectives; see [§ Numbers](#Numbers).

*First- and second-declension adjectives* are declined like first-declension nouns for the feminine forms and like second-declension nouns for the masculine and neuter forms. For example, for *mortuus, mortua, mortuum* 'dead', *mortua* is declined like a regular first-declension noun (such as *puella* 'girl', *mortuus* is declined like a regular second-declension masculine noun (such as *dominus* 'lord, master', and *mortuum* is declined like a regular second-declension neuter noun (such as *auxilium* 'help'.

*Third-declension adjectives* are mostly declined like normal third-declension nouns, with a few exceptions. In the plural nominative neuter, for example, the ending is *-ia* (*omnia* 'all, everything', and for third-declension nouns, the plural nominative neuter ending is *-a* or *-ia* (*capita* 'heads', *animalia* 'animals'. They can have one, two or three forms for the masculine, feminine, and neuter nominative singular.

#### Participles

Latin participles, like English participles, are formed from a verb. There are a few main types of participles: Present Active Participles, Perfect Passive Participles, Future Active Participles, and Future Passive Participles.
